preamble: The shift from the voiceless alveolar stop [t] to the voiced sibilant [z] (typically through an intermediate [s] or [ts] stage) is a major feature of the High German Consonant Shift. It involves affrication followed by spirantization and voicing, marking a significant divergence between High German and other Germanic languages.
phoneticEffects: Assibilation, Spirantization, High German Consonant Shift

examples.note: The initial stop affricated into a sibilant-onset sound.
Intervocalic stops shifted to full sibilants.
Synchronic affrication of initial /t/ in stressed syllables.

sources: Waterman: A History of the German Language
